title: Shortest Unique Substring for Each Word
description: Find smallest unique substring per word

examples.explanation: For "cat": "c" is unique. For "dog": "d" is unique. For "apple": "a" appears in "cat" (freq=2), "p" appears multiple times within word (freq=2), "l" and "e" appear once (freq=1). Shortest unique is "l" (or "e"). Output ["c", "d", "l"].
For "aa": "a" appears in both words (freq=3), "aa" appears only in first word (freq=1). For "ab": "a" shared (freq=3), "b" only in second word (freq=1). Shortest unique: "aa" and "b".

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n * m^3)
approaches.summary: For each word, generate all possible substrings and check uniqueness across all words by comparing to substrings of other words. Use this as a baseline to understand the problem constraints.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n * m^3) where n is number of words and m is max word length

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Shortest Unique Substring
ladders.desc: Complete implementation finding smallest unique substring for each word by generating all substrings and checking uniqueness across all words.
ladders.explanation: This brute force approach solves the problem by exhaustively checking all possible substrings for each word to find the smallest one that doesn't appear in any other word. The intuition is straightforward: for each word, we generate every possible substring (from length 1 to the full word length), then check if that substring exists in any other word's substrings. We start with the shortest substrings and work our way up, ensuring we find the smallest unique substring first. The algorithm works by first building a complete set of all substrings from all words, then for each word, we iterate through its substrings from shortest to longest, checking if each substring appears in the global set more than once (counting occurrences across all words). The first substring that appears exactly once (only in its source word) is the answer for that word. Time complexity is O(n * m³) because for n words with max length m, we generate O(m²) substrings per word, and checking uniqueness involves comparing against O(n * m²) total substrings. Space complexity is O(n * m²) to store all substrings. This approach serves as a baseline to understand the problem's constraints before optimizing. Common mistakes include not considering overlapping substrings properly, forgetting to handle empty inputs, and inefficient substring generation that misses some possibilities. The connection to optimization approaches is that this brute force method clearly demonstrates why more efficient solutions are needed for large inputs.

examples.trace: Build frequency map of all substrings. For "cat": "c" appears once → unique. For "dog": "d" appears once. For "apple": "l" appears once → unique. Output ["c", "d", "l"].
Build frequency map: "a" appears in both words (freq=3), "aa" only in first word (freq=1), "b" only in second word (freq=1). For "aa": "aa" is unique. For "ab": "b" is unique. Output ["aa", "b"].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Substring Uniqueness Checker
ladders.desc: Check if a given substring appears uniquely across all words' substrings.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the brute force approach: determining whether a specific substring from one word appears in any other word. The intuition is that we need to compare a candidate substring against all possible substrings from all other words to verify uniqueness. The algorithm takes a target substring and a collection of all words (or precomputed substring sets), then checks if this substring exists in any word other than its source. We can implement this by first generating all substrings from all words and storing them in a frequency dictionary, then simply looking up the target substring's frequency. If the frequency is exactly 1, the substring is unique to its source word. Time complexity for a single check is O(1) if we have precomputed all substrings, but building the frequency map takes O(n * m²). Space complexity is O(n * m²) for storing the frequency map. This connects to the full problem by being the critical validation step that determines when we've found the shortest unique substring. Common mistakes include not properly handling multiple occurrences within the same word (which should count as multiple entries in the frequency map), forgetting to exclude the source word when checking (though frequency=1 handles this), and inefficient re-computation of substrings for each check. The real pattern involves building a complete substring frequency dictionary once, then using it for all uniqueness checks.

examples.trace: Step 1: Generate all substrings. For "abc": "a", "b", "c", "ab", "bc", "abc". For "cab": "c", "a", "b", "ca", "ab", "cab". For "abx": "a", "b", "x", "ab", "bx", "abx". Step 2: Count frequencies: "ab" appears in "abc", "cab", and "abx" → frequency = 3. Step 3: Check if target "ab" is unique for source word "abc" (index 0): frequency = 3 > 1, so not unique → return false.

Step 1: Generate substrings. For "abc": "a", "b", "c", "ab", "bc", "abc". For "xyz": "x", "y", "z", "xy", "yz", "xyz". For "def": "d", "e", "f", "de", "ef", "def". Step 2: Frequency count: "xyz" appears only in word "xyz" → frequency = 1. Step 3: Check uniqueness for source word "xyz" (index 1): frequency = 1 → unique → return true.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Generating All Substrings for a Word
ladders.desc: Generate every possible substring of a given string systematically.
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves creating all contiguous subsequences of a string, which is fundamental to the brute force approach. The intuition is that to find unique substrings, we must consider every possible substring candidate. The algorithm uses nested loops: an outer loop for the starting index (from 0 to length-1) and an inner loop for the ending index (from start to length-1). For each (start, end) pair, we extract the substring from start to end+1. This generates substrings of all lengths from 1 to the full word length. We typically collect these in a list or set. Time complexity for one word of length m is O(m²) because there are m(m+1)/2 ≈ m²/2 substrings. Space complexity is O(m²) to store them. This connects to the uniqueness checker by providing the pool of substrings to check against. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in loop boundaries, generating non-contiguous sequences (which are not substrings), forgetting to include single-character substrings, and inefficient string slicing that creates unnecessary copies. The real pattern is straightforward nested iteration with string slicing.

examples.trace: Step 1: Length = 3. Start outer loop i from 0 to 2. Step 2: i=0: inner loop j from 0 to 2: j=0 → substring[0:1]="a", j=1 → "ab", j=2 → "abc". Step 3: i=1: j from 1 to 2: j=1 → "b", j=2 → "bc". Step 4: i=2: j from 2 to 2: j=2 → "c". Step 5: Collect all: ["a", "ab", "abc", "b", "bc", "c"]. Sort by length then lexicographically for clarity: ["a", "b", "c", "ab", "bc", "abc"].
Step 1: Length = 2. i from 0 to 1. Step 2: i=0: j from 0 to 1: j=0 → "a", j=1 → "ab". Step 3: i=1: j from 1 to 1: j=1 → "b". Step 4: Result: ["a", "ab", "b"]. Sorted: ["a", "b", "ab"].

ladders.title: Building Block — Frequency Counting of Substrings
ladders.desc: Count how many times each substring appears across multiple word collections.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves creating a frequency dictionary (hash map) that maps each substring to how many times it occurs across all generated substrings from all words. The intuition is that to quickly check uniqueness, we need to know if a substring appears more than once. The algorithm iterates through all substrings from all words, using a dictionary to increment counts for each substring. For multiple occurrences within the same word, each is counted separately (e.g., "aa" has two "a" substrings). Time complexity is O(total substrings) = O(n * m²). Space complexity is O(unique substrings) which is at most O(n * m²). This connects to the uniqueness checker by providing the data structure for O(1) lookup. Common mistakes include using a set instead of a dictionary (losing count information), not counting duplicates within a word, and inefficient updates. The real pattern is simple dictionary accumulation.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize empty dict {}. Step 2: Process first list ["a", "b"]: add "a" → dict{"a":1}, add "b" → dict{"a":1, "b":1}. Step 3: Process second list ["a", "c"]: add "a" → dict{"a":2, "b":1}, add "c" → dict{"a":2, "b":1, "c":1}. Step 4: Final frequency dict.
Step 1: Initialize {}. Step 2: First list: "ab"→1, "bc"→1. Step 3: Second list: "ab"→2, "cd"→1. Step 4: Final dict.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — String Slicing to Extract Substring
ladders.desc: Extract a contiguous portion of a string given start and end indices.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves retrieving a substring from a string using start and end indices. The intuition is that strings are sequences of characters, and we can access a range using slicing. The algorithm uses string slicing syntax: string[start:end] returns characters from index start (inclusive) to end (exclusive). For example, "hello"[1:4] gives "ell". Time complexity is O(k) where k is the length of the substring, as it may create a new string copy. Space complexity is O(k) for the new string. This connects to substring generation by being the fundamental way to obtain each candidate substring. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors (remembering end is exclusive), using negative indices incorrectly, and assuming slicing works the same in all programming languages (some use start/length instead). The real pattern is straightforward indexing.

examples.trace: Step 1: String indices: 0:p, 1:r, 2:o, 3:g, 4:r, 5:a, 6:m, 7:m, 8:i, 9:n, 10:g. Step 2: Slice from index 3 (inclusive) to 7 (exclusive): indices 3,4,5,6 → characters g,r,a,m. Step 3: Result: "gram".
Step 1: Indices: 0:a,1:b,2:c,3:d,4:e. Step 2: Slice 1 to 4: indices 1,2,3 → b,c,d. Step 3: Result: "bcd".

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Strings as Character Sequences
ladders.desc: Basic concept of strings being indexed sequences of characters.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept establishes that strings are ordered collections of characters where each character has a position index starting from 0. The intuition is that computers represent text as sequences, allowing access to individual characters and operations like slicing. The algorithm involves understanding zero-based indexing: for a string of length L, valid indices are 0 through L-1. We can access a single character using string[index], and get the length using len(string). Time complexity for access is O(1) typically. Space complexity for a string is O(L) to store L characters. This connects to string slicing by providing the underlying indexed structure. Common mistakes include thinking indices start at 1, confusing length with last index (last index is length-1), and not understanding that strings are immutable in many languages. The real pattern is treating strings like arrays of characters.

examples.trace: Step 1: String has 5 characters. Step 2: Index 0 → first character 'h'. Step 3: Index 1 → second character 'e'. Step 4: Index 2 → third character 'l'. Step 5: Index 3 → fourth character 'l'. Step 6: Index 4 → fifth character 'o'. Step 7: Length = 5, so valid indices are 0,1,2,3,4.
Step 1: Length = 3. Step 2: Index 0 → 'a'. Step 3: Index 1 → 'b'. Step 4: Index 2 → 'c'. Step 5: No index 3 (out of bounds).

approaches.name: Trie with Suffixes O(n * m^2)
approaches.summary: Build a trie containing all substrings from all words, then for each word, traverse the trie to find the shortest substring with a count of 1. This leverages prefix structures to avoid redundant comparisons.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n * m^2) for inserting all substrings into the trie

ladders.title: Full Problem — Shortest Unique Substring for Each Word Using Trie with Suffixes
ladders.desc: Complete solution finding smallest unique substring per word by inserting all substrings into a trie and querying counts.
ladders.explanation: The intuition behind this approach is that by storing all possible substrings from all words in a trie, we create a global frequency map of substring occurrences. For each word, we can then efficiently find the shortest substring that appears exactly once across all words by traversing the trie. This works because the trie organizes substrings by their prefixes, allowing us to check uniqueness without redundant comparisons. The algorithm proceeds in two main phases: first, building the trie by inserting every substring from every word (for each word, generate all substrings starting at each position and insert them into the trie, incrementing node counts). Second, for each word, generate its substrings in order of increasing length, and for each substring, traverse the trie to check if its count is 1—the first such substring found is the shortest unique one. The time complexity is O(n * m^2) where n is the number of words and m is the maximum word length, due to generating O(m^2) substrings per word and inserting each into the trie in O(m) time. Space complexity is also O(n * m^2) for storing all substrings in the trie. Connection to L1: This full solution relies on the key subroutine of inserting substrings into the trie and querying counts. Common mistakes include not handling empty words correctly, forgetting to reset traversal for each substring query, or inefficiently generating substrings. Real code pattern: Define a TrieNode class with children dictionary and count; insert all substrings via nested loops; for each word, iterate over substring lengths from 1 to word length, and for each starting index, traverse trie to check count—return first with count 1.

examples.trace: Step 1: Build trie with all substrings. For "cat": substrings "c", "ca", "cat", "a", "at", "t". Insert each, incrementing counts. Similarly for "bat" and "rat". After insertion, trie nodes have counts: "c" count=1, "b" count=1, "r" count=1, "a" appears in multiple substrings so count>1, etc. Step 2: Query each word. For "cat": check substrings in order: "c" has count=1, so output "c". For "bat": "b" count=1, output "b". For "rat": "r" count=1, output "r".

Step 1: Build trie. All words are identical, so all substrings (e.g., "d", "do", "dog", "o", "og", "g") have count=3 each. Step 2: Query each word. For first "dog": check "d" count=3, "do" count=3, "dog" count=3, "o" count=3, "og" count=3, "g" count=3—no substring has count=1, so output empty string. Same for other words.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Insert All Substrings into Trie and Query Count
ladders.desc: Implement trie operations to insert substrings and check if a substring has count 1.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the full solution, focusing on the trie data structure operations. The intuition is that a trie allows efficient storage and retrieval of strings by their prefixes, and by maintaining a count at each node, we can track how many times a substring (represented by the path from root to that node) appears across all words. The algorithm involves two functions: insert_substring(word) which takes a string and inserts all its substrings into the trie by iterating over starting indices and lengths, traversing or creating nodes, and incrementing counts; and query_substring(substring) which traverses the trie following the substring's characters and returns the count at the final node. Time complexity for insertion is O(m^2) per word for generating and inserting substrings, and query is O(k) for a substring of length k. Space complexity is O(total substrings) for trie nodes. Connection to L0: This sub-routine is called repeatedly in the full problem to build the trie and check uniqueness. Common mistakes include not initializing counts properly, handling missing nodes in queries, or inefficient substring generation. Real code pattern: TrieNode with children dict and count; insert_substring loops i from 0 to len(word)-1 and j from i to len(word)-1, traversing trie for word[i:j+1], incrementing count; query_substring traverses trie for given string, returns count or 0 if path doesn't exist.

examples.input: Insert substrings of "ab" into empty trie, then query "a" and "ab"
examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize trie root. Step 2: Insert substrings of "ab": start i=0, j=0: substring "a", traverse: root->'a', create node, count=1. i=0, j=1: substring "ab", traverse: root->'a'->'b', create node, count=1. i=1, j=1: substring "b", traverse: root->'b', create node, count=1. Step 3: Query "a": traverse root->'a', node exists, count=1. Query "ab": traverse root->'a'->'b', count=1.

examples.input: Insert substrings of "aa" into empty trie, then query "a" and "aa"
examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize trie root. Step 2: Insert substrings of "aa": i=0, j=0: "a", traverse root->'a', create node, count=1. i=0, j=1: "aa", traverse root->'a'->'a', create node, count=1. i=1, j=1: "a", traverse root->'a', node exists, increment count to 2. Step 3: Query "a": count=2. Query "aa": count=1.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Using Trie to Track Substring Frequencies
ladders.desc: Concept of maintaining counts in a trie to determine substring uniqueness.
ladders.explanation: The central insight is that a trie can serve as a frequency map for strings, where each node represents a prefix, and the count at a node indicates how many times the complete substring (from root to that node) appears. This is powerful because it allows us to check uniqueness in O(L) time for a substring of length L, compared to O(n*m) if we compared against all other words. The intuition is that by inserting all substrings, we aggregate global knowledge, and querying becomes a simple traversal. The algorithm involves designing a TrieNode with children pointers and a count integer. When inserting a substring, we traverse the trie character by character, creating nodes as needed, and increment the count at the final node. To check if a substring is unique, we traverse to its node and see if count == 1. Time complexity for insertion and query depends on substring length, but overall efficient for many operations. Space complexity is proportional to the number of distinct substrings stored. Connection to L1: This logic is implemented in the sub-routine functions. Common mistakes include not resetting counts when reusing trie, or misunderstanding that counts are per substring not per prefix. Real code pattern: class TrieNode: def __init__(self): self.children = {}; self.count = 0. Insert: node = root; for char in substring: if char not in node.children: node.children[char] = TrieNode(); node = node.children[char]; node.count += 1. Query: similar traversal, return node.count if exists else 0.

examples.input: Trie with counts after inserting "ab" and "ac" substrings
examples.output: Node for "a" has count=2, "ab" count=1, "ac" count=1
examples.trace: Step 1: Insert substrings of "ab": "a" count=1, "ab" count=1, "b" count=1. Step 2: Insert substrings of "ac": "a" traverse to existing node, increment count to 2; "ac" create new path, count=1; "c" count=1. Result: root->'a' count=2, ->'b' count=1, ->'c' count=1; also direct 'b' and 'c' nodes from root.

examples.input: Check uniqueness for substring "xy" in trie with "xy" inserted once and "xyz" inserted
examples.trace: Step 1: Insert "xy" substrings: "x" count=1, "xy" count=1, "y" count=1. Step 2: Insert "xyz" substrings: "x" increment count to 2, "xy" increment count to 2, "xyz" count=1, "y" increment count to 2, "yz" count=1, "z" count=1. Step 3: Query "xy": traverse root->'x'->'y', node count=2, so not unique. Wait, correction: in this example, "xy" appears in both "xy" and "xyz" substrings, so count=2, not unique. Let's redo: Input was "xy" inserted once and "xyz" inserted, so "xy" substring appears from "xy" and from "xyz" (as prefix), so count=2. Output should be not unique. Revised trace: After insertions, "xy" node has count=2. Query returns 2, so not unique.

ladders.title: Building Block — Generating All Substrings of a String
ladders.desc: Algorithm to produce every possible substring from a given string.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is essential because the trie approach requires inserting all substrings to compute frequencies. The intuition is that a substring is defined by a starting index and an ending index within the string. By iterating over all possible pairs of indices, we can generate every substring. The algorithm uses nested loops: the outer loop iterates over starting indices from 0 to len(s)-1, and the inner loop iterates over ending indices from the starting index to len(s)-1. For each pair, the substring is s[start:end+1]. This generates substrings in no particular order, but for the full problem, we often need to process them in increasing length order when querying. Time complexity is O(m^2) for a string of length m, as there are m*(m+1)/2 substrings. Space complexity is O(m^2) if storing all substrings, but in the trie approach, we process them on the fly. Connection to L2: This substring generation feeds into the trie insertion process. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in indices, generating duplicates, or inefficiency. Real code pattern: substrings = []; for i in range(len(s)): for j in range(i, len(s)): substrings.append(s[i:j+1]). For the full problem, we might iterate lengths from 1 to m and starts from 0 to m-length for ordered generation.

examples.trace: Step 1: Start i=0: j=0: substring "a"; j=1: "ab"; j=2: "abc". Step 2: i=1: j=1: "b"; j=2: "bc". Step 3: i=2: j=2: "c". Total 6 substrings.

Step 1: i=0: j=0: "a"; j=1: "aa". Step 2: i=1: j=1: "a". Note: "a" appears twice as substring from different starts, but in set terms, it's one unique substring. For trie insertion, both are inserted, increasing count.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Trie Insertion and Traversal
ladders.desc: Fundamental operations to add a string to a trie and check its presence.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves manipulating a trie data structure, which is a tree where each node represents a character, and paths from the root spell out strings. The intuition is that tries allow efficient prefix-based operations. For insertion, we start at the root and for each character in the string, we move to the corresponding child node, creating it if it doesn't exist. After processing all characters, we mark the end node (often with a count or flag). For traversal (or search), we similarly follow the characters, returning success if we reach the end without missing nodes. In the context of the problem, we extend this by adding a count at each node to track frequencies. Time complexity for both operations is O(L) where L is string length. Space complexity is O(N * L) in worst case for distinct strings. Connection to L3: This operation is used when inserting generated substrings into the trie. Common mistakes include not handling empty strings, forgetting to create nodes, or incorrect traversal logic. Real code pattern: class TrieNode: def __init__(self): self.children = {}; self.count = 0. Insert: node = root; for char in string: if char not in node.children: node.children[char] = TrieNode(); node = node.children[char]; node.count += 1. Traverse: node = root; for char in string: if char not in node.children: return None; node = node.children[char]; return node.

examples.input: Insert "cat" into empty trie, then traverse "ca"
examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize root. Step 2: Insert "cat": char 'c': create node; 'a': create node; 't': create node; increment count at 't' node to 1. Step 3: Traverse "ca": start at root, follow 'c' node, then 'a' node, return node for 'a'.
Step 1: Insert "dog": create nodes for 'd', 'o', 'g'. Step 2: Traverse "cat": start at root, 'c' not in root's children, so return None.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Strings and Substrings
ladders.desc: Basic definitions and operations on strings and their substrings.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept is essential for beginners to grasp the problem. A string is a sequence of characters, such as "hello". A substring is a contiguous sequence of characters within a string. For example, from "hello", substrings include "h", "he", "hel", etc., but not "hl" (non-contiguous). The intuition is that substrings are defined by a starting index and an ending index in the string. Key operations include accessing characters by index (e.g., s[0] for first character), slicing to get substrings (e.g., s[1:3] for characters from index 1 to 2), and finding length. In programming, strings are often zero-indexed. This concept connects to L4 as we use these operations to generate substrings for trie insertion. Common mistakes include confusion between substrings and subsequences, off-by-one errors in slicing, or not handling empty strings. Real code pattern: In Python, s = "abc"; s[0] is 'a'; s[0:2] is "ab" (indices 0 to 1); len(s) is 3. To get all substrings, we use loops over indices.

examples.trace: Step 1: Identify string "dog" with indices 0:'d', 1:'o', 2:'g'. Step 2: Substrings: start at 0: length 1: "d"; length 2: "do"; length 3: "dog". Start at 1: length 1: "o"; length 2: "og". Start at 2: length 1: "g". Total 6 substrings.
Step 1: String "a" has length 1. Step 2: Only substring is from start 0, length 1: "a".

approaches.name: HashMap of Substrings O(n * m^2)
approaches.summary: Use a hashmap to count occurrences of all substrings across words, then for each word, iterate through its substrings in increasing length order to find the first with a count of 1. This approach simplifies lookup with hashing.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n * m^2) for generating and hashing all substrings

ladders.title: Full Problem — Shortest Unique Substring for Each Word Using HashMap
ladders.desc: Find the smallest unique substring for each word in a list by counting all substrings across words.
ladders.explanation: The intuition behind this approach is that to find the shortest unique substring for each word, we need to know which substrings appear only once across all words. By generating all possible substrings from every word and counting their occurrences in a hashmap, we create a frequency dictionary. Then, for each word, we iterate through its substrings in increasing length order (from length 1 to the word's length) and check the hashmap: the first substring with a count of 1 is the shortest unique one. This works because shorter substrings are checked first, ensuring minimal length. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Initialize an empty hashmap 'substring_count'. 2. For each word in the input list: generate all substrings (using nested loops for start and end indices) and increment their count in the hashmap. 3. For each word: iterate substring lengths from 1 to word length; for each length, generate substrings and check if count is 1 in hashmap; return the first such substring. 4. If no unique substring exists (e.g., all substrings are shared), return the whole word. Time complexity is O(n * m^2) where n is number of words and m is average word length, due to generating O(m^2) substrings per word. Space complexity is O(n * m^2) for storing substrings in the hashmap. This feeds directly from L1 by applying the substring counting helper to solve the full problem. Common mistakes include not handling empty inputs, forgetting to reset substring generation per word, or missing edge cases where no unique substring exists. Real code pattern: def shortest_unique_substrings(words): substring_count = {} for word in words: for i in range(len(word)): for j in range(i+1, len(word)+1): sub = word[i:j]; substring_count[sub] = substring_count.get(sub, 0) + 1 result = [] for word in words: found = False for length in range(1, len(word)+1): if found: break for i in range(len(word)-length+1): sub = word[i:i+length]; if substring_count[sub] == 1: result.append(sub); found = True; break if not found: result.append(word) return result

examples.trace: Step 1: Count all substrings. For 'apple': substrings include 'a','p','p','l','e','ap','pp','pl','le','app','ppl','ple','appl','pple','apple'. For 'app': 'a','p','p','ap','pp','app'. For 'apricot': many substrings including 'c'. Step 2: Build hashmap counts. For example, 'a' appears in all words, count=3; 'p' appears in first two, count=2; 'appl' appears only in 'apple', count=1; 'c' appears only in 'apricot', count=1. Step 3: Find shortest unique per word. For 'apple': check length 1 substrings: 'a'(3),'p'(2),'p'(2),'l'(1) but 'l' is not unique? Wait, 'l' appears only in 'apple', so count=1? Actually, 'l' is substring of 'apple' only, so yes count=1. But we need shortest: 'l' length 1, but is it unique? Check: 'l' does not appear in 'app' or 'apricot', so count=1. So output 'l'? But example says 'appl'. Let's trace properly: For 'apple', substrings: at length 1: 'a'(3),'p'(2),'p'(2),'l'(1),'e'(1). 'l' has count 1, so shortest unique is 'l'. But wait, the problem might require substring starting from any position? Yes, 'l' is valid. But in example output is 'appl'. I need to re-check: Actually, in the full problem, we iterate substrings in increasing length order, so for 'apple', we check all length 1 substrings first: 'a','p','p','l','e'. 'l' has count 1? Let's verify counts: 'l' appears only in 'apple', so count=1. So 'l' should be output. But example says 'appl'. There might be a mistake. Let's correct: For 'apple', shortest unique substring is 'l' or 'e'? 'e' also count 1. So either 'l' or 'e'. But to match example, I'll use correct trace. Actually, in the problem, we need smallest unique substring, and if multiple, any is fine? But example shows 'appl'. I think my initial example is flawed. Let's use a correct example. Input: ['dog','cat','car']. Step 1: Count substrings. 'dog': 'd','o','g','do','og','dog'. 'cat': 'c','a','t','ca','at','cat'. 'car': 'c','a','r','ca','ar','car'. Step 2: Hashmap: 'd':1,'o':1,'g':1,'do':1,'og':1,'dog':1,'c':2,'a':2,'t':1,'ca':2,'at':1,'cat':1,'r':1,'ar':1,'car':1. Step 3: For 'dog': check length 1: 'd'(1) so output 'd'. For 'cat': length 1: 'c'(2),'a'(2),'t'(1) so output 't'. For 'car': length 1: 'c'(2),'a'(2),'r'(1) so output 'r'. Output: ['d','t','r']. So I'll use this as example 1. For example 2: Input: ['a','aa','aaa']. Step 1: Count: 'a': substrings 'a' count 3. 'aa': 'a','a','aa' so 'a' count increases, 'aa' count 1. 'aaa': 'a','a','a','aa','aa','aaa' so 'a' count increases, 'aa' count increases, 'aaa' count 1. Hashmap: 'a':6,'aa':3,'aaa':1. Step 2: For 'a': length 1: 'a'(6) not unique, no shorter, so output 'a'. For 'aa': length 1: 'a'(6), length 2: 'aa'(3) not unique, so output 'aa'. For 'aaa': length 1: 'a'(6), length 2: 'aa'(3), length 3: 'aaa'(1) so output 'aaa'. Output: ['a','aa','aaa']. So I'll update examples accordingly.

Step 1: Generate all substrings. For 'a': substrings ['a']. For 'aa': substrings ['a','a','aa']. For 'aaa': substrings ['a','a','a','aa','aa','aaa']. Step 2: Count in hashmap. Initialize empty. Process 'a': 'a' count becomes 1. Process 'aa': 'a' count becomes 1+2=3, 'aa' count becomes 1. Process 'aaa': 'a' count becomes 3+3=6, 'aa' count becomes 1+2=3, 'aaa' count becomes 1. Final counts: {'a':6,'aa':3,'aaa':1}. Step 3: Find shortest unique per word. For word 'a': iterate lengths from 1 to 1. Length 1: substring 'a' has count 6, not 1. No unique substring, so output whole word 'a'. For word 'aa': lengths 1 to 2. Length 1: substrings 'a' (count 6), 'a' (count 6). Length 2: substring 'aa' (count 3). None have count 1, so output 'aa'. For word 'aaa': lengths 1 to 3. Length 1: 'a' (6), 'a' (6), 'a' (6). Length 2: 'aa' (3), 'aa' (3). Length 3: 'aaa' (1). So 'aaa' has count 1, output 'aaa'. Final result: ['a','aa','aaa'].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Count All Substrings Across Words
ladders.desc: Generate all substrings from a list of words and count their frequencies using a hashmap.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that to identify unique substrings later, we first need a global frequency map of every substring across all words. This sub-routine efficiently builds this map by iterating through each word and extracting all possible contiguous substrings. It works because a hashmap provides O(1) average-time lookups and updates, allowing us to accumulate counts as we generate substrings. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Initialize an empty dictionary 'counts'. 2. For each word in the input list: a. For each start index i from 0 to len(word)-1: b. For each end index j from i+1 to len(word): c. Extract substring = word[i:j]. d. Increment counts[substring] by 1 (or set to 1 if not present). 3. Return the counts dictionary. Time complexity is O(n * m^2) where n is number of words and m is average length, since each word has O(m^2) substrings. Space complexity is O(n * m^2) for storing substrings in the hashmap. This feeds into L0 by providing the frequency data needed to find unique substrings. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in substring indices, not handling empty words, or using inefficient data structures. Real code pattern: def count_substrings(words): counts = {} for word in words: for i in range(len(word)): for j in range(i+1, len(word)+1): sub = word[i:j]; counts[sub] = counts.get(sub, 0) + 1 return counts

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize counts = {}. Step 2: Process first word 'ab'. i=0: j=1: substring 'a', counts{'a':1}. j=2: substring 'ab', counts{'a':1,'ab':1}. i=1: j=2: substring 'b', counts{'a':1,'ab':1,'b':1}. Step 3: Process second word 'bc'. i=0: j=1: substring 'b', counts{'a':1,'ab':1,'b':2}. j=2: substring 'bc', counts{'a':1,'ab':1,'b':2,'bc':1}. i=1: j=2: substring 'c', counts{'a':1,'ab':1,'b':2,'bc':1,'c':1}. Final counts: {'a':1,'b':2,'c':1,'ab':1,'bc':1}.
Step 1: counts = {}. Step 2: Process word 'x'. i=0: j=1: substring 'x', counts{'x':1}. No more indices. Final counts: {'x':1}.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Find Shortest Unique Substring for a Single Word
ladders.desc: Given a word and a hashmap of substring counts, find the smallest substring that appears only once.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that uniqueness is determined by the global frequency count, and we want the shortest such substring to minimize length. By checking substrings in increasing length order, we guarantee the first match is the shortest. This works because we iterate from length 1 upward, and for each length, we generate all substrings of that length and query the hashmap for a count of 1. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Input: a word 'w' and a dictionary 'counts' from L1. 2. For length L from 1 to len(w): a. For start index i from 0 to len(w)-L: b. substring = w[i:i+L]. c. If counts[substring] == 1, return substring. 3. If no unique substring found, return the whole word w. Time complexity is O(m^2) in worst case as we check up to O(m^2) substrings. Space complexity is O(1) extra space. This feeds into L1 by being applied to each word after counting substrings. Common mistakes include not handling the case where no unique substring exists, or incorrectly assuming counts keys exist for all substrings. Real code pattern: def shortest_unique(word, counts): for L in range(1, len(word)+1): for i in range(len(word)-L+1): sub = word[i:i+L]; if counts.get(sub, 0) == 1: return sub return word

examples.trace: Step 1: word='apple', len=5. Step 2: L=1: i=0: sub='a', counts['a']=3 not 1. i=1: sub='p', counts['p']=2 not 1. i=2: sub='p', counts['p']=2 not 1. i=3: sub='l', counts['l']=1, so return 'l'. Output 'l'.

Step 1: word='test', len=4. Step 2: L=1: i=0: 't' count 2, i=1: 'e' count 2, i=2: 's' count 2, i=3: 't' count 2. L=2: i=0: 'te' count 2, i=1: 'es' count 2, i=2: 'st' count 2. L=3: i=0: 'tes' count 2, i=1: 'est' count 2. L=4: i=0: 'test' count 2. No substring has count 1, so return whole word 'test'.

ladders.title: Building Block — Generate All Substrings of a String
ladders.desc: Extract every contiguous substring from a given string using nested loops.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that substrings are defined by a start index and an end index, so we can systematically iterate over all possible pairs to generate them. This building block is essential because it provides the set of substrings needed for counting and uniqueness checks. It works by using two pointers: the outer loop sets the start position, and the inner loop extends the end position to capture substrings of increasing length from that start. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Input: a string s. 2. Initialize an empty list 'substrings' (or process directly). 3. For i from 0 to len(s)-1: a. For j from i+1 to len(s): b. substring = s[i:j]. c. Add substring to list or yield it. 4. Return the list of substrings. Time complexity is O(m^2) where m is string length, as there are m*(m+1)/2 substrings. Space complexity is O(m^2) for storing all substrings. This feeds into L2 by providing the substrings to check for uniqueness. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in loop bounds, or generating non-contiguous sequences. Real code pattern: def all_substrings(s): result = []; for i in range(len(s)): for j in range(i+1, len(s)+1): result.append(s[i:j]); return result

examples.trace: Step 1: s='ab', len=2. Step 2: i=0: j=1: substring 'a'. j=2: substring 'ab'. i=1: j=2: substring 'b'. Result: ['a','ab','b'].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Hashmap Increment for Substring Counting
ladders.desc: Update a hashmap to count frequencies of strings using get-or-default operations.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that a hashmap (dictionary) allows efficient storage and retrieval of key-value pairs, where keys are substrings and values are their counts. This operation is fundamental because it accumulates frequencies as we process substrings. It works by checking if a key exists in the map: if yes, increment its value; if no, set it to 1. This ensures accurate counting without pre-initializing all possible keys. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Input: a hashmap 'counts' and a string 'key'. 2. If key is in counts: counts[key] += 1. 3. Else: counts[key] = 1. 4. Return updated counts. Time complexity is O(1) average for hashmap operations. Space complexity is O(1) extra per key. This feeds into L3 by being used each time a substring is generated. Common mistakes include using incorrect hashmap methods or not handling empty keys. Real code pattern: def increment_count(counts, key): if key in counts: counts[key] += 1 else: counts[key] = 1 return counts

examples.trace: Step 1: counts={'a':1}, key='b'. Step 2: Check if 'b' in counts: no. Step 3: Set counts['b']=1. Step 4: Result {'a':1,'b':1}.
Step 1: counts={'x':2}, key='x'. Step 2: Check if 'x' in counts: yes. Step 3: Increment counts['x'] to 3. Step 4: Result {'x':3}.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — String Slicing and Hashmap Basics
ladders.desc: Understand how to extract substrings and use dictionaries for key-value storage.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that strings are sequences of characters, and slicing allows us to access contiguous parts efficiently. Hashmaps (dictionaries) provide a way to associate keys with values, enabling fast lookups. This foundation is crucial because the entire approach relies on manipulating substrings and counting them. String slicing works by specifying start and end indices: s[start:end] returns characters from index start up to but not including end. If indices are out of bounds, it handles gracefully. Hashmap basics involve operations like insertion, retrieval, and update. For example, in Python, a dictionary can be created with {}, and you can set or get values using square brackets. The step-by-step for slicing: 1. Given string s='hello', s[0:2] returns 'he'. 2. For hashmap: dict = {}; dict['key'] = 1; value = dict.get('key', 0). Time complexity for slicing is O(k) where k is slice length; for hashmap operations, O(1) average. Space complexity depends on data stored. This feeds into L4 by providing the tools to generate substrings and count them. Common mistakes include confusing slice indices or misusing hashmap methods. Real code pattern: s = 'example'; substring = s[1:4]  # returns 'xam'. counts = {}; counts['a'] = 1; counts['a'] += 1.

examples.trace: Step 1: s='programming', indices: 0:p,1:r,2:o,3:g,4:r,5:a,6:m,7:m,8:i,9:n,10:g. Step 2: start=3, end=7. Step 3: Extract characters from index 3 to 6 (since end is exclusive): indices 3:g,4:r,5:a,6:m. Step 4: Result 'gram'.

examples.input: Hashmap: Initialize dict, set key 'count' to 5, then increment by 1
examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize empty dict: dict = {}. Step 2: Set dict['count'] = 5. Step 3: dict now {'count':5}. Step 4: Increment: dict['count'] += 1, so dict['count'] becomes 6. Step 5: Result {'count':6}.

testCases.input: Hashmap: dict={}, get key 'missing' with default 0

approaches.name: Rolling Hash with Binary Search O(n * m * log m)
approaches.summary: For each word, use binary search on substring length combined with rolling hashes to quickly check uniqueness via hash sets, reducing the need to generate all substrings explicitly. This optimizes by minimizing substring comparisons.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n * m * log m) due to binary search and rolling hash checks
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n * m) for storing hash sets during checks

ladders.title: Full Problem — Shortest Unique Substring for Each Word using Rolling Hash with Binary Search
ladders.desc: Complete solution finding smallest unique substring per word using binary search on length with rolling hash uniqueness checks.
ladders.explanation: This solution solves the problem efficiently by combining binary search with rolling hashes. For each word, we perform binary search on possible substring lengths (1 to m). At each length L, we generate all substrings of length L using rolling hash, store their hashes in a global set, and check for collisions. The smallest L where all substrings of that word have unique hashes (not appearing in any other word) gives us the shortest unique substring length. We then find the actual substring by checking all substrings of that length. The intuition is that binary search reduces the O(m²) substring generation to O(log m) checks, while rolling hash allows O(1) substring hash computation and O(1) set lookups. The algorithm: 1) For each word, binary search L from 1 to len(word). 2) For each L, generate all substring hashes using rolling hash. 3) Check if any hash appears in other words' substring hashes at same L. 4) If all unique, move left; else move right. 5) After finding minimal L, find first substring of that length with unique hash. Time: O(n * m * log m) where n=words, m=max length. Each binary search iteration processes O(m) substrings with O(1) hash operations. Space: O(n * m) for storing hash sets during checks. Connection to L1: Uses the uniqueness checker as core subroutine. Common mistakes: Not handling hash collisions (need double hash or actual string comparison), incorrect binary search bounds, forgetting to reset hash sets between lengths. Real code pattern: Use polynomial rolling hash with large prime modulus, binary search with while left<right, maintain dictionary mapping (hash,word_index) to track ownership.

examples.trace: Word 0 "cat": Binary search lengths 1-3. L=2: Generate hashes for "ca", "at". Check global: No other words have length 2 substrings yet. All unique → move left to L=1. L=1: Generate hashes for "c", "a", "t". Check global: "c" appears in "car" ("c"), "a" appears in "car" ("a") and "bat" ("a"), "t" unique. Not all unique → move right to L=2 (found minimal). Find first unique substring at L=2: "ca" hash not in any other word → output "ca". Word 1 "car": L=2: "ca" collides with "cat", "ar" unique → not all unique. L=3: "car" unique → all unique. L=2.5 → L=3 minimal. First unique substring at L=3: "car" → output "car". Word 2 "bat": L=1: "b" unique, "a" collides with "car", "t" collides with "cat" → not all unique. L=2: "ba" unique, "at" collides with "cat" → not all unique. L=3: "bat" unique → all unique. L=2.5 → L=3 minimal. First unique substring at L=3: "bat" → output "bat". Final: ["ca", "car", "bat"] but actually shortest unique: "ca" (cat), "ar" (car), "b" (bat). Re-check: For "car", L=2: "ar" is unique ("ca" not in other words at L=2? Wait "ca" in "cat" at L=2, so "ar" is only unique). So minimal L=2, first unique substring "ar". For "bat", L=1: "b" is unique (others: "a" collides, "t" collides). So minimal L=1, first unique substring "b". Correct output: ["ca", "ar", "b"].

Word 0 "dog": L=2: "do" unique, "og" unique → all unique. L=1: "d" collides with "dot", "o" collides with "dot" and "lot", "g" unique → not all unique. Minimal L=2, first unique "do". Word 1 "dot": L=1: "d" collides with "dog", "o" collides with "dog" and "lot", "t" unique → not all unique. L=2: "do" collides with "dog", "ot" unique → not all unique. L=3: "dot" unique → all unique. Minimal L=3, first unique "dot" but check L=1: "t" is actually unique ("t" not in other words at L=1? "dog" has "g", "lot" has "l","o","t" → "t" appears in "lot"! So "t" collides). L=2: "ot" unique ("ot" not in others). So minimal L=2, first unique "ot"? Wait need first: substrings "do","ot". "do" collides, "ot" unique → output "ot". But example says "t" - error. Let's recompute: For "dot", L=1 substrings: "d","o","t". Check others: "dog" has "d","o","g"; "lot" has "l","o","t". So "t" appears in "lot" → collides. L=2: "do","ot". "do" appears in "dog" → collides. "ot" not in others → unique. So minimal L=2, first unique "ot". Word 2 "lot": L=1: "l" unique, "o" collides, "t" collides → not all unique. L=2: "lo" unique, "ot" collides with "dot" → not all unique. L=3: "lot" unique → all unique. Minimal L=3, first unique "lot". But L=1: "l" is unique ("l" not in others). So minimal L=1, first unique "l". Correct output: ["do", "ot", "l"]. Example output has "t" which is wrong. Use correct: ["do", "ot", "l"].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Check Uniqueness of All Substrings of Given Length Using Rolling Hash
ladders.desc: Determine if all substrings of specified length in a word have unique hashes not appearing in other words.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the core of the efficient solution. Given a word and a target length L, we need to check whether every substring of length L in this word has a hash value that doesn't appear in any substring of length L from other words. The intuition is that by using rolling hash, we can compute all substring hashes in O(m) time instead of O(m²), and by using hash sets, we can check uniqueness in O(1) time per substring. The algorithm: 1) Compute rolling hash for all substrings of length L in the current word. 2) For each hash, check if it exists in a global dictionary that maps hashes to word indices. 3) If a hash already exists and was from a different word, then this substring is not unique. 4) If all hashes are unique (either not in dictionary or from same word), return True; else False. Time: O(m) where m is word length, since we compute m-L+1 substrings with O(1) hash updates. Space: O(n * m) worst-case for storing all hashes, but during this check we only need O(m) for current word's hashes. Connection to L0: This function is called during binary search to test candidate lengths. Common mistakes: Not handling collisions (two different substrings having same hash) - should use double hash or fallback to string comparison. Not considering that same substring within same word should be considered non-unique if appears multiple times. Real code pattern: Use polynomial rolling hash with base and modulus, precompute powers, sliding window to compute hashes, maintain set of seen hashes from all words.

examples.trace: Word "apple" length 5. Generate substrings length 2: "ap", "pp", "pl", "le". Compute hashes (simplified): "ap" hash exists in other_hashes from word 0 → collision with different word. So not all unique. Return False.
Word "bat" length 3. Generate substring length 3: only "bat". Compute hash. other_hashes is empty, so no collisions. Return True.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Binary Search on Substring Length for Minimal Unique Substring
ladders.desc: Use binary search to find the smallest length L where all substrings of length L in a word are unique across all words.
ladders.explanation: This is the optimization insight that reduces the problem from checking all possible lengths (1 to m) to O(log m) checks. The intuition is that uniqueness is monotonic with respect to substring length: if all substrings of length L are unique, then all substrings of longer lengths (L+1, L+2, ...) will also be unique (because longer substrings are more specific). Conversely, if substrings of length L are not all unique, then shorter lengths (<L) will also not be all unique (because shorter substrings are more common). This monotonic property allows binary search. The algorithm: 1) Set left=1, right=len(word). 2) While left < right: mid = (left+right)//2. 3) Check if all substrings of length mid are unique (using rolling hash check). 4) If True, set right=mid (search for smaller length). 5) If False, set left=mid+1 (need longer length). 6) After loop, left is the minimal length where uniqueness holds. Time: O(log m) checks, each check O(m) with rolling hash, so O(m log m) per word. Space: O(m) for hash computation during checks. Connection to L1: Uses the uniqueness checker at each binary search step. Common mistakes: Incorrect binary search bounds (off-by-one), not handling case where no unique substring exists (but in this problem, the whole word is always unique). Real code pattern: Standard binary search with while left<right, adjusting bounds based on checker result.

examples.trace: left=1, right=5. mid=3: Check uniqueness for L=3. Substrings: "app","ppl","ple". Suppose "app" appears in another word → not unique. So False → left=4. mid=4: L=4: "appl","pple". Both unique? Suppose yes → True → right=4. left=4, right=4 → loop ends. Minimal L=4. But wait, need to trace properly: Actually for "apple", shortest unique might be shorter. Let's assume: L=1: "a","p","p","l","e" - "p" repeated within word → not all unique. L=2: "ap","pp","pl","le" - "pp" unique? Actually "pp" appears only here. But need check across words. Suppose other words: "app" has "ap","pp". So "ap" and "pp" collide → not all unique. L=3: "app","ppl","ple" - "app" collides with "app" from other word → not all unique. L=4: "appl","pple" - both unique. L=5: "apple" unique. Binary search: left=1,right=5,mid=3 → False → left=4. mid=4 → True → right=4. Done. Output 4.

left=1, right=3. mid=2: Check L=2. Substrings: "ca","at". Suppose both unique across words → True → right=2. left=1, right=2, mid=1: Check L=1. "c","a","t". Suppose "a" collides → False → left=2. left=2, right=2 → done. Output 2.

testCases.input: word="a", uniqueness_checker returns True for L=1
word="ab", checker: L=1 False, L=2 True
word="abc", checker: L=1 False, L=2 False, L=3 True
word="aaaa", checker: L=1 False (all 'a'), L=2 False (all 'aa'), L=3 False, L=4 True
word="test", checker: L=1 False, L=2 True
word="hello", checker: L=1 False, L=2 False, L=3 True
word="python", checker: L=1 False, L=2 False, L=3 False, L=4 True
word="ababa", checker: L=1 False, L=2 False, L=3 True
word="unique", checker: L=1 False, L=2 False, L=3 False, L=4 False, L=5 False, L=6 True

ladders.title: Building Block — Rolling Hash for Efficient Substring Hash Computation
ladders.desc: Compute hash values for all substrings of fixed length using sliding window polynomial hash.
ladders.explanation: Rolling hash allows computing hash values for consecutive substrings in O(1) time after O(m) preprocessing, instead of O(m) per substring. The intuition is to treat strings as numbers in a base (e.g., 26 for lowercase letters) and use polynomial hash: hash(s) = (s[0]*p^(L-1) + s[1]*p^(L-2) + ... + s[L-1]) mod M, where p is a prime base and M is a large prime modulus. For a sliding window of length L, when moving from substring s[i:i+L] to s[i+1:i+L+1], we can update the hash as: new_hash = ((old_hash - s[i]*p^(L-1)) * p + s[i+L]) mod M. This works because we subtract the contribution of the leaving character (multiplied by appropriate power) and add the new character. The algorithm: 1) Precompute powers of p modulo M up to length m. 2) Compute initial hash for first L characters. 3) For i from 1 to m-L: new_hash = ((old_hash - char_val[i-1]*p_pow[L-1]) * p + char_val[i+L-1]) mod M. Adjust with +M if negative. Time: O(m) for all substrings of fixed L, O(1) per update. Space: O(m) for precomputed powers. Connection to L2: Provides the efficient hash computation needed for the uniqueness checker. Common mistakes: Integer overflow (use modulo operations), incorrect power computation, not handling negative values in modulo. Real code pattern: Precompute p_pow array, convert characters to integers (ord(c)-ord('a')), compute initial hash with loop, then sliding window updates.

examples.trace: Convert to numbers: a=1,b=2,c=3. Precompute p_pow: p_pow[0]=1, p_pow[1]=31. Initial hash for "ab": (1*31^1 + 2*31^0) mod M = (31+2)=33. Next window: old_hash=33, leave char 'a'=1, enter char 'c'=3. new_hash = ((33 - 1*31^1) * 31 + 3) mod M = ((33-31)*31+3) = (2*31+3)=65. So hashes: 33 ("ab"), 65 ("bc").

All chars a=1. Initial hash for first 3: (1*31^2 + 1*31^1 + 1*31^0) = 961+31+1=993. Next window: old_hash=993, leave a=1, enter a=1. new_hash = ((993 - 1*31^2) * 31 + 1) = ((993-961)*31+1) = (32*31+1)=993. So both hashes 993.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Polynomial String Hashing
ladders.desc: Compute a numeric hash value for a string using polynomial rolling hash function.
ladders.explanation: Polynomial hashing converts a string into a large integer by treating it as a polynomial with characters as coefficients. The hash is computed as: hash(s) = (s[0]*p^(k-1) + s[1]*p^(k-2) + ... + s[k-1]) mod M, where s[i] is the integer value of the character (e.g., ord(c)-ord('a') for lowercase), p is a prime base (typically 31 for lowercase letters), and M is a large prime modulus (e.g., 10^9+7). The intuition is that this creates a unique fingerprint for the string with high probability, and the polynomial structure allows efficient updates for sliding windows. The algorithm: 1) Initialize hash=0. 2) For each character in string: hash = (hash * p + char_value) mod M. This builds the hash from left to right, effectively computing the polynomial. Alternatively, can compute with powers explicitly. Time: O(k) for string of length k. Space: O(1). Connection to L3: This is the fundamental hash computation used in rolling hash updates. Common mistakes: Using small p or M causing collisions, not using modulo leading to overflow, inconsistent character mapping. Real code pattern: Loop through characters, update hash with hash = (hash * base + val) % mod.

examples.trace: b=2, a=1. hash=0. hash = (0*31+2)=2. hash = (2*31+1)=63. So hash=63. Different from "ab" (33), good.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — String Substrings and Uniqueness
ladders.desc: Understand what substrings are and how to check if they are unique within a set.
ladders.explanation: A substring is a contiguous sequence of characters within a string. For example, in "cat", the substrings are "c", "a", "t", "ca", "at", "cat". The uniqueness of a substring means that it does not appear anywhere else in a given collection of strings. For example, "ca" is unique in ["cat", "car"] if "ca" appears only in "cat" and not in "car". The intuition is that we want to find the shortest such substring for each word. The basic operation is to generate all substrings of a word and check for duplicates. The algorithm naively: 1) For each word, generate all substrings (start index i from 0 to len-1, end index j from i+1 to len). 2) For each substring, check if it appears in any other word. 3) Keep track of the shortest unique substring. Time: O(n * m^3) if comparing strings directly, because O(m^2) substrings per word, each comparison O(m). Space: O(n * m^2) to store all substrings. Connection to L4: This naive approach is optimized by using hashing to compare substrings in O(1) time. Common mistakes: Confusing subsequence (non-contiguous) with substring, not considering empty substring, off-by-one in indices. Real code pattern: Nested loops for start and end indices, extract substring with slicing, use set to track seen substrings.

examples.trace: Generate substrings of "ab": "a", "b", "ab". Check uniqueness: "a" appears in "ac" → not unique. "b" does not appear in "ac" → unique. "ab" does not appear in "ac" → unique but longer. Shortest unique is "b".
Substrings: "o", "o", "oo". "o" appears in other word "o" → not unique. "oo" does not appear in "o" → unique. So shortest unique is "oo".
